---
title: "US Weekly Gas Price Volatility"
subtitle: "INFO 526 - Summer 2026 - Final Project"
author: "Elizabeth Ladd"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/watercolour_sys02_img34_teacup-ocean.jpg
  data-background-size: stretch
  data-background-opacity: "0.7"
  data-slide-number: none
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:  ['data/customtheming.scss']
  
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{r}
#| label: load-packages
#| include: false

# Load packages here
pacman::p_load(tidymodels,
               tidyverse,
               dplyr,
               ggplot2,
               lubridate,
               ggrepel,
               slider,
               stringr)

```

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Plot theme
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11))

# For better figure resolution
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  fig.retina = 3, 
  dpi = 300, 
  fig.width = 6, 
  fig.asp = 0.618 
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: load-data
#| include: false
# Load data here
weekly_gas_prices <- readr::read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-07-01/weekly_gas_prices.csv")
# code source: https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-07-01/readme.md

VIX_History <- read_csv("data/VIX_History.csv")
# data source: https://www.cboe.com/tradable-products/vix/vix-historical-data/
```

# Background

## **Why Study Gas Price Volatility?**

- **Gas prices surged above \$4/gallon nationwide** in spring 2026, the highest levels since 2022.[^1]

- **Geopolitical events and supply disruptions** (particularly conflict affecting Middle East oil supplies and the Strait of Hormuz) triggered rapid week-to-week swings in crude oil and gasoline prices.

- This raises an interesting question: How unusual are large gasoline price spikes, and how has price volatility changed over the past 35 years?

[^1]: Source: [Gas prices hit \$4 a gallon; Kuwaiti tanker struck off Dubai - The Washington Post](https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/2026/03/31/gas-prices-four-dollars/)

## Historical Dataset

- The dataset is from the TidyTuesday github repo from 2025-07-01. It looks at the weekly US gas prices for both regular and diesel. The date range of this data set is from Aug 1990 to June 2025.

- "The data comes from the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), which publishes average retail gasoline and diesel prices each Monday."[^2]

[^2]: Source: [Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update - U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA)](https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/gasdiesel/ "https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/gasdiesel/")

## Data

The raw data from TidyTuesday has multiple rows for each week for fuel type (gasoline/diesel), grade (regular, premium, etc) and formulation (conventional, all)

```{r, echo=FALSE}
weekly_gas_prices |>    
  head() |>   
  kableExtra::kable() |>   
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```

# Question 1: Are sudden spikes in gas prices common?

## Data Manipulation: Filter

First, filter the data to avoid counting the same week multiple times.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#filters to avoid counting the same week multiple times
gas_filter <- weekly_gas_prices %>%
  filter(
    fuel == "gasoline",
    grade == "regular",
    formulation == "all"
  ) %>%
  arrange(date)

gas_filter %>% glimpse()

```

## Data Manipulation: Weekly Change Calculation

Then calculate the weekly change and add a column.

```{r, echo=FALSE}

#calculates weekly change
gas_change <- gas_filter %>%
  mutate(
    weekly_change = price - lag(price),
    pct_change = 100 * (price - lag(price)) / lag(price)
  )

# Remove the first row because there was no previous data for a change
gas_change <- gas_change[-1, ]

glimpse(gas_change)

```

## Histogram Plot

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#plots histogram
ggplot(gas_change, aes(x = weekly_change)) +
  geom_histogram(
    binwidth = 0.02,
    fill = "steelblue",
    color = "white"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Weekly Gas Price Changes",
    x = "Weekly Change ($)",
    y = "Number of Weeks"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```

## Data Manipulation: Spike Determination

Finally, calculate the top 1, 5 and 10% changes and add a column to determine if it is considered a spike.

Result:

```{r, echo=FALSE}


#adds column to determine if it is a spike week: top 1, 5 and 10%
gas_change_spike <- gas_change |>
  mutate(
    percentile = percent_rank(weekly_change),
    spike_level = case_when(
      percentile >= 0.99 ~ "Top 1%",
      percentile >= 0.95 ~ "Top 5%",
      percentile >= 0.90 ~ "Top 10%",
      TRUE ~ "Normal"
    )
  )

#convert 'spike_level' to factor and specify level order
gas_change_spike$spike_level <- 
  factor(gas_change_spike$spike_level, 
         levels=c('Top 1%' ,
                  'Top 5%',
                  'Top 10%',
                  'Normal'
         )
  )

table(gas_change_spike$spike_level)


```

## Spike Highlight on Time Series Plot

```{r, echo=FALSE}

ggplot(gas_change_spike, aes(date, price)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(
    data = filter(gas_change_spike, spike_level != "Normal"),
    aes(
      color = spike_level,
      size = spike_level)
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Top 1%" = "red",
      "Top 5%" = "orange",
      "Top 10%" = "gold"
    ), 
    name = "Spike Level"
  )+
  scale_size_manual(
    values = c(
      "Top 10%" = 2,
      "Top 5%" = 3,
      "Top 1%" = 4
    ),
    guide = "none"
  )+
  labs(
    title = "Gas Prices with Major Weekly Spikes Highlighted",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Price",
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```

# Question 2: Does stock market uncertainty coincide with increased gas prices?

## Data Mining: Stock Market

Utilize the Volatility Index VIX from Cboe

```{r, echo=FALSE}
VIX_History |>    
  head() |>   
  kableExtra::kable() |>   
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```

## Data Manipulation: Stock Market

Then clean the data to match the gas price data and group by weeks and get the mean

```{r, echo=FALSE}

#clean VIX Data: change to lowercase, makes dates in right format and selects just the data and close columns
vix_clean <- VIX_History |>
  rename_with(tolower) |>
  mutate(
    date = mdy(date)
  ) |>
  select(date, vix = close)

#converts vix data from daily data to weekly means to match the gas
vix_weekly <- vix_clean %>%
  mutate(week = floor_date(date, unit = "week", week_start = 1)) |>
  group_by(week) |>
  summarize(
    vix = mean(vix, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

vix_weekly |>
  head() |>   
  kableExtra::kable() |>   
  kableExtra::kable_styling()

```

## Data Manipulation: Gas Price Volatility and Scaling

Then calculate the gas volatility and join with the VIX data, then scale to compare.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
gas_weekly <- gas_filter |>
  mutate(
    week = floor_date(date, unit = "week", week_start = 1),
    pct_change = 100 * (price - lag(price)) / lag(price)
  )

#calculates volitility to match VIX
gas_weekly <- gas_weekly |>
  mutate(
    gas_volatility = slide_dbl(
      pct_change,
      sd,
      .before = 25,
      .complete = TRUE,
      na.rm = TRUE
    )
  )

#joins together
gas_vix_join <- gas_weekly|>
  inner_join(vix_weekly, by = "week") |>
  filter(!is.na(gas_volatility))
  
 
gas_vix_scaled <- gas_vix_join |>
  mutate(
    vix_scaled = as.numeric(scale(vix)),
    gas_vol_scaled = as.numeric(scale(gas_volatility))
  )

gas_vix_scaled <-  gas_vix_scaled |>
  select(week, gas_volatility, vix, vix_scaled, gas_vol_scaled)

gas_vix_scaled |>
  head() |>   
  kableExtra::kable() |>   
  kableExtra::kable_styling()

```

## Plot

```{r, echo=FALSE}
ggplot(gas_vix_scaled, aes(x = week)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = vix_scaled, color = "Stock Market Volatility")) +
  geom_line(aes(y = gas_vol_scaled, color = "Gasoline Volatility")) +
  labs(
    title = "Stock Market and Gasoline Price Volatility Over Time",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Standardized value",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Question 3: Are gas price spikes associated with major geopolitical or supply-disruption events?

## Data: 1% Filter

First, filter to find the top 1% to get an idea of when the top 1% of spikes occurred to guide research.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#filters to find the top 1% 
top_one_filter <- gas_change_spike |>   
  filter(spike_level == "Top 1%") |>
  select(date, pct_change, spike_level)
         
top_one_filter   |>
  head() |>   
  kableExtra::kable() |>   
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```

## Data: Global Events

Create a data set of global events that could impact gas prices.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
events <- tribble(
  ~event_date, ~event, ~type,
  "2001-09-11", "9/11 attacks", "Geopolitical",
  "2003-03-20", "Venezuelan general strikes", "Supply/Demand",
  "2003-03-20", "Iraq War begins", "Geopolitical",
  "2005-08-29", "Hurricane Katrina", "Weather/Supply",
  "2008-07-01", "Oil price shock", "Market",
  "2010-12-18", "Arab Spring begins", "Geopolitical",
  "2020-03-13", "COVID declared", "Supply/Demand",
  "2021-03-23", "Suez Canal blockage", "Supply/Demand",
  "2022-02-24", "Russia invades Ukraine", "Geopolitical"
) |>
  mutate(event_date = as.Date(event_date))

events   |>
  head() |>   
  kableExtra::kable() |>   
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
```

## Plot

```{r, echo=FALSE}
event_windows <- gas_change_spike |>
  crossing(events) |>
  filter(
    date >= event_date - weeks(8),
    date <= event_date + weeks(8)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    weeks_from_event = as.numeric(difftime(date, event_date, units = "weeks")),
    event = str_wrap(event, width = 15)
  )

ggplot(event_windows, aes(x = weeks_from_event, y = pct_change)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "grey50") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(aes(color = spike_level), size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~ event, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Normal" = "grey70",
      "Top 10%" = "gold",
      "Top 5%" = "orange",
      "Top 1%" = "red"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Gasoline Price Changes Around Major Events",
    subtitle = "Eight weeks before and after each event",
    x = "Weeks from event",
    y = "Weekly percent change",
    color = "Spike level"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## **Conclusions**

1.  Question 1: Are sudden spikes in gas prices common?

    - Conclusion: No, no change from week to week is the most common, with only a small percentage falling in the "spike" category.

2.  Question 2: Does stock market uncertainty coincide with increased gas prices?

    - Conclusion: There are some instances when volatility in gas prices coincides with volatility in the stock market. But there are other instances where they behave opposite of one another.

3.  Bonus Question 3: Are gas price spikes associated with major geopolitical or supply-disruption events?

    - Conclusion: More analysis and data collection would be needed for a more definitive conclusion, but for a rough estimate shows that top 1% spikes occured within an 8 week window of Hurricane Katrina, the 2008 oil price shock and Russia invading Ukraine.

## References

1.  [Gas prices hit \$4 a gallon; Kuwaiti tanker struck off Dubai - The Washington Post](https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/2026/03/31/gas-prices-four-dollars/)

2.  [Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update - U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA)](https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/gasdiesel/ "https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/gasdiesel/")

3.  The event data set was generated by providing chat GPT the following prompt: "Provide a list of global events that could impact US gas prices from 1990-2025 and the date they occurred"
